conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I have the most accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few more questions.

Do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes, but Alex doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you at the moment, I need to understand your situation a bit better.

Are you contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or their accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep. I help out with things like clothes, food, and other essentials.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. To assess your eligibility properly, I need to understand the amount you're contributing.

Are you contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards Alex's upkeep? (Child Benefit is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, or £16.95 per week for additional children - so for Alex as your only child, it would be £25.60 per week or about £111 per month)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm contributing more than that. I give £30 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Where is Alex currently living? For example, is Alex living with another family member, a friend, in care, or in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living with their other parent - my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Now I need to know one more important thing:

Has anyone else (such as your ex-partner) already claimed Child Benefit for Alex, or are you the only one claiming/intending to claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. I'm the one wanting to claim it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Just to confirm a couple of final details:

Is Alex under 16 years old, or if they're 16-19, are they in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is 8 years old, so under 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Just one final question to complete the assessment:

Are there any other circumstances I should know about, such as Alex being in hospital or care for an extended period, or any local council payments being made towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nothing like that. Alex just lives with their other parent, and I contribute towards their upkeep each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement

✓ Although Alex doesn't live with you, you're contributing £30 per week towards their upkeep (for clothes, food, and other essentials)

✓ This contribution exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child, which means you're considered responsible for Alex under the rules

✓ No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

According to the government rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you either live with them OR pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since you're contributing more than the Child Benefit amount, you meet this requirement.

**Important note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. You can still claim Child Benefit, but you might have to pay back some or all of it through your tax return.

You can now proceed to apply for Child Benefit if you haven't already done so.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16, meeting the age requirement for Child Benefit. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant is contributing £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child. According to the eligibility rules, a person normally qualifies for Child Benefit if they're responsible for a child under 16 and either live with them OR are paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money. The claimant meets this requirement by contributing more than the Child Benefit amount. Additionally, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. There are no other complicating factors such as care arrangements or local council payments. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child, Alex. Although Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, the claimant contributes £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the required Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week. This makes the claimant responsible for Alex under the Child Benefit rules. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and there are no other circumstances that would affect eligibility.

There are no other complicating factors such as care arrangements or local council payments. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
